Now, after everybody has generated his or her ideas through private thinking, it's time to move to step three, which is to solicit input. Have you ever been in a meeting and had a great idea but nobody listened carefully to it? It's not uncommon. This sort of thing is not only frustrating and demotivating, it's also a lost opportunity for the whole group, which misses great ideas that could turn an influence challenge from a pain to a gain. We advise that all group members be given a turn to pitch their best ideas. Now, although we may like the others in our group, we don't have time to listen to their full life stories in every meeting, so our advice is to give each group member a maximum of two and a half minutes to pitch only their best ideas. We also recommend that when one person is talking, everyone else remains silent. It's not yet time for even well meant feedback. For that, please wait for step four. We advise to arrange for a timekeeper and a note taker before anyone starts talking. And one last piece of advice. If there's a hierarchy in the room, start with the input of the lowest in rank and move up. It shows that if the CEO, for example, takes the floor first, people will refer to his or her ideas instead of sharing their own. That's another way to miss out on good ideas.
